data.title: GE Aerospace: Long-Term Growth, Short-Term Risk
data.description:
GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) is one of the aerospace and defense companies that I have been bullish on. In January, I attached a $239 price target to the name. The stock price, however, has declined 7.8% since then. In comparison, the S&P 500 (SP500) declined 13.7% since then. It is not the kind of performance we are looking for in a name with a buy rating. However, perhaps the lower decline that GE Aerospace showed somewhat reflects its resilience.

In this report, I will discuss the risks that the company faces in the current trade environment. Additionally, I will discuss how the earnings and free cash flow estimates have developed and update my price target for GE.

GE Aerospace Business Leans On Long-Term Trends

GE Aerospace leans on long-term demand for airplanes and thus airplane engines and associated services. Typically, the airplane engines do not come with high margins. However, the services have a very attractive margin over the longer term, as prices for life-limited parts or LLPs typically are escalated at a rate higher than inflation. Through this escalation cadence, GE Aerospace is shielded against inflation, labor cost increases and raw material costs. Aero engine prices are typically also escalated similarly.

While the business is shielded through its escalation principle, tariffs can still increase the cost of goods and a bigger risk is that it can disrupt the aerospace supply chain, which has been fragile for years. Additionally, in the case GDP growth slows down, there might be lower demand for new airplanes. Typically, demand for airplanes is supported by 2-3 percent in GDP growth. GDP growth is expected to be 2.7% in 2025-2026 and that is below the long-term average of 3.1% and the current trade turmoil may adversely impact GDP growth. The European Central Bank already revised its GDP outlook down by 20 basis points in March. Vanguard has lowered the economic growth outlook for the US to below 1% and growth outlooks for China have also been trimmed. So, there is substantial pressure on the GDP outlook that could subsequently result in lower capacity growth for air travel and affect demand for new aero engines and services.

Marginally Lower Estimates For GE Aerospace

For GE Aerospace, we see that EBITDA estimates for 2025 have been lower by 0.6% and 0.8% in the year after. This results in a total downward revision of 0.4% between FY 2025 and FY 2027. Free cash flow is expected to be 1.4% than previously estimated, but 3.7% lower in FY 2026, for a total reduction in FCF estimates of 0.9%. Those are estimate changes that don’t really move the needle in my opinion.

GE Aerospace Has Upside Despite Lower Peer Group Valuation Multiple

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While EBITDA and free cash flow estimates did not come down substantially, I am adjusting my price target from $239 to $206.25. This is not driven by the earnings growth, but by the lower trading multiple for the aerospace and defense industry.

I do believe in the prospects of GE Aerospace, but at this point it is fair to account for the lower EV/EBITDA of the peer group and value accordingly. In that scenario, there remains 14% upside and another 13% in the year after. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating for GE Aerospace.

Conclusion: GE Aerospace Remains Attractive

GE Aerospace is not cheap; it is fairly valued with FY 2025 earnings in mind, and that also was the case in my assessment in January. However, I do believe that the lower stock price does offer an entry position to incrementally add on market weakness with the long-term prospects of the company in mind.

As a result, I am reducing my price target in line with a lower peer group valuation but reiterate my buy rating.

data.title: Amazon Turns Challenges Into Opportunities: Why I Am Buying
data.description:
Investing When The Market Drops

In this article, I want to focus on Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and explain why I bought more of it and will continue doing so. Although some may call it a no-brainer or a screaming buy, I think we should avoid similar ways of saying when we buy shares. It may be easier to come to a buy rating in certain circumstances, but we are, in any case, using our rational intelligence, which we should always consider our best asset. So, I think Amazon well deserves some thoughts, which I want to share in this article.

Whipsaw scenarios dominate the financial scene nowadays. We have seen the market trade down and up double digits in just one day. We have rallies that give some relief to battered bulls, and then bears seem to emerge once again. Who knows where the market is going? Nobody, unless the answer is: somewhere. But we know that the likelihood of the market being far higher ten years from now is considerable.

This answer clashes with what investors want: safety and certainty. And yet, we also know that opportunities arise when the clouds loom on the horizon or, in other words, when risk is perceived. There are plenty of papers and interesting pieces of research on panic selling and how it leads investors to mistakes such as selling assets prematurely, ignoring fundamentals, overreacting, herd behavior, and avoiding re-entering the market. But I think many of us have felt fear in the past few days and have at least thought about selling or exiting the market. After all, if we have been invested for a few years, we are probably sitting on quite large gains that we would like to preserve.

This is when having a strategy and sticking to it pays off. Therefore, even though I have no clue whether the market will head north or south this year, I consider what is happening right now as a buying opportunity.

Now, Aswath Damodaran, to this extent, has argued that "the data seems to point more to a markdown in equity values than to panic selling". He showed how investors respond to a crisis by affecting the price they pay for assets, demanding higher risk premiums. He also explains that deeming every market dip as a buying opportunity can be due to intellectual laziness. What I am doing right now is due to the fact that I have a macroeconomic view that thinks this crisis could lead to a stronger Western bloc. Why I think so can lead me astray from the purpose of this article, but I think North America and Europe will eventually have to be strictly bonded to be of the same size as China and India.

In any case, right now, we could profit from the situation by simply buying the market. But I am picking some stocks I consider to be all-weather options that are currently being erroneously discounted. In any case, it is key to know what we are buying.

Think about the example we have heard so many times: if you buy a nice home you feel comfortable living in, will you sell it if the home market tanks? Probably not. Well, I am currently after stocks. I would like to hold on to, just as I want to hold on to my nice home with a sea view, I was able to buy for my family (here is a picture I took a few days ago at sunrise; having very young kids has the "advantage" of early wake-ups).

Now, enough with behavioral finance and personal examples. What am I buying right now? At the time of writing, I am seeing the market plummet by over 5% after one of its best days, where many stocks gained +10%.

Personally, I believe this is just one of those rallies that are typically seen during bear markets. We are reading all over the place how two similar days with double-digit gains happened in October 2008, as the Great Financial Crisis was spreading.

So, yes. I am buying stocks. But to paint the whole picture, I have to give more information. In the past year and a half, I have stacked up some cash as I was finding it more and more difficult to invest in convincing buys. I kept buying stocks, but it was harder and harder to find good deals. So, I took advantage of 5% yielding savings accounts and increased my deposits. Secondly, I am not deploying in the market everything I saved all at once. This is because bear markets can last a year, but sometimes can be longer. Since no one knows when it will end, it is better to step in gradually and keep consistency in dollar-cost-averaging.

To be fully transparent, I am not buying only Amazon, but I have also been actively buying Ferrari (RACE), I have doubled my position in Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMY), and a new entry in my portfolio: United Rentals (URI). I have not sold a single share of any stock.

Why Amazon

My investment in Amazon began when the release of the first season of The Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power was about to be released in September 2022. We were seeing Amazon plummet day after day. I started picking it up at around $120 and bought the sell-off until it bottomed in the $80s. If you want to read something to divert your attention from tariffs and the current market bloodshed, you may find my research interesting. I was trying to figure out why Amazon could deploy well over $1B just for this series. What I found out was something extremely valuable about Amazon's economics. In short, I focused on how Amazon wants to attract new members into its Prime ecosystem, and I showed how this membership program was highly influenced by Costco's business model. Back in 2022, we estimated that every member who signs up for Amazon Prime spends its estimated lifetime value for Amazon was over $1,300. This meant that with just 759k new Prime members, Amazon was obtaining in present lifetime value an amount similar to its investment for the first two seasons. Probably, the new lifetime value of a Prime member is now higher, in addition, Amazon has also raised its membership fee. So, the economics of this ecosystem are probably getting better. In short, I believed I understood why Amazon always wins.

But I also started focusing on Amazon's strive to be a company of inventors. In particular, I showed how it is probably the only company able to challenge Nvidia's dominance in AI chips. The company stays true to the old saying that someone's margin is someone else's opportunity.

However, because of its relentless inventive nature, Amazon's fundamentals don't always please investors. Let's look at its earnings and its FCF. Since the pandemic, for example, we have witnessed two massive investment cycles. The first one started in late 2020 and was meant to increase Amazon's fulfillment capacity with new warehouses, given the rapid surge of its online sales. Investors saw Amazon's FCF go well into the negative (up to a $30B cash burn). Its earnings too plummeted. As a result, Amazon's valuation multiples were extremely poor.

But then Amazon switched into profit mode and rapidly proved to the market that it can easily make over $50B in FCF. The chart would have probably kept exploding if AI hadn't come about. But since Amazon rapidly saw the opportunity of this revolution, it is now investing deeply to build AI-supportive infrastructure and technologies that will be embedded in its ecosystem. The partnership with Anthropic is key to this extent.

Last, but not least, Amazon's profitable AWS business is now supported by a rapidly growing segment, which is Amazon Ads. I consider Amazon to be the best digital advertising company because no other company I know can advertise so effectively.

What about China?

Right now, the issue concerning investors with Amazon is its relationship with China. But I think we often assume it is far larger than we expect. In short, it mainly affects the retail business. As we will see, if the retail business takes a hit, Amazon's profit margins could actually move higher.

As Amazon explains in its 2024 Annual Report (bold is mine):

The People's Republic of China (PRC) and India regulate Amazon's and its affiliates' businesses and operations in country through regulations and license requirements that may restrict (i) foreign investment in and operation of the internet, IT infrastructure, data centers, retail, delivery, and other sectors, (ii) internet content, and (iii) the sale of media and other products and services. For example, in order to meet local ownership, regulatory licensing, and cybersecurity requirements, we provide certain technology services in China through contractual relationships with third parties that hold PRC licenses to provide services. [...] In addition, because China-based sellers account for significant portions of our third-party seller services and advertising revenues, and China-based suppliers provide significant portions of our components and finished goods, regulatory and trade restrictions, data protection and cybersecurity laws, economic factors, geopolitical events, security issues, or other factors negatively impacting China-based sellers and suppliers could adversely affect our operating results.

Amazon doesn't disclose what percentage of its third-party sellers are from China or Hong Kong. I have read several estimates, and they range between 60% to 70%. This is a sensible amount. As we can see, China is more critical to Amazon's supply chain than to its sales. In fact, net sales attributable to countries that represent a significant portion of Amazon's sales don't include China.

Amazon does over two-thirds of its sales in the U.S., which is over 10x the size of the second-largest market, which is Germany. After all, we know that the Chinese market is dominated by Alibaba (BABA).

But let's now understand where this piece of information finds its context.

In 2024, Amazon grew its total revenue by 11% to $638B. It has three segments: North America, International, and AWS. Respectively, they were up 10% to $387B, 9% to $143B, 19% to $108B. Just ten years ago, AWS did only $4.8B, and Amazon's total revenue was below $90B. Adj. Free Cash Flow reached $36.2B.

Now, let's look at the company's net sales by category. Online stores represent $247B. Third-party seller services are the second-largest category, with a revenue of $156B.

Now, Amazon doesn't disclose its margins by category. We know that AWS's margins often exceed 30%. For the retail business, the number we usually consider correct is 6-7%. The ads business can be compared to Google's, which reports an operating margin close to 40%.

Therefore, Amazon is exposed to the current tariff war for two reasons: difficulties in replacing its Chinese suppliers, and lower ad revenue.

However, I think we can also consider that some trends could be triggered to offset part of the impact. In particular, the advertising business could see some growth from new third-party sellers or higher-priced goods sellers that want to reposition themselves and take market share from Chinese suppliers that are pulling back.

There could, however, be the overall impact of a recession. Not even Amazon is immune to this.

Now, what I would like to show is that if third-party sellers diminish, Amazon could take a hit in online sales, but this would make AWS weigh more, making the company more profitable. Moreover, Chinese suppliers could be replaced by other higher-tier suppliers that are affected by lower tariffs. Think about Turkey, for example, or India. There could also be an influx of new suppliers, as demand for many goods manufactured in China is expected to remain. These new suppliers would need to make themselves known on Amazon and could spend on advertising, offsetting the negative impact of fewer Chinese suppliers.

In other words, I think Amazon's ecosystem is so big that it will eventually rebalance its operations.

Valuation

As I said, Amazon's valuation can fluctuate quite a bit due to how Amazon prefers to invest big when it finds an opportunity, rather than outlining a smooth upward trajectory of its earnings. However, right now, we are seeing Amazon invest convincingly while also proving that it is a cash flow-generating machine. As a result, we have some multiples that reflect Amazon's valuation while the company is doing a bit of everything, so to speak.

We have a fwd PE below 30, 28.6 to be precise, which is almost unprecedented. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is around 12, which seems cheap by Amazon's standards. Even its P/FCF is interesting, with a 12.5 multiple. Considering Amazon could well do $70B in FCF if it paused its AI-related capex, we could have this multiple cut in half.

Could Amazon trade down? Yes, but the valuation would only become more and more attractive for a company that is deeply entrenched in the U.S. and the Western world. But, given the structure of its operations, I consider it one of the best safe havens in the market. Although it may be more volatile than defensive stocks, what is truly defensive of Amazon is its ecosystem and the structure of its business, which will help it sail through this crisis as it has with the others, it faced since its founding.

data.title: AMD: Look Beyond Tariffs And Focus On The Big Picture
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) has seen great volatility, but I am of the view that a lot of the AI hype has left the balloon and investors can focus solely on the fundamentals. Wall Street appears to be punishing the stock due to the competitive gap versus Nvidia (NVDA), which appears to only be getting wider and wider. I have no arguments against that narrative, as the numbers do appear to imply that NVDA continues to assert dominance over the GPU market. However, following what I view to be an excessive selloff due to tariffs, at current valuations I’d argue that AMD can remain a distant second and still present a compelling investment. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

AMD Stock Price

I last covered AMD in January, when I named it a top pick even in spite of being “second fiddle.” The stock has retreated nearly 20% since then.

Investors may need to be patient with this one due to the thesis hinging on a shift in sentiment, but I see the numbers eventually validating this story.

AMD Stock Key Metrics

AMD has fallen behind in the generative AI race. This company was once regarded as closely following NVDA, but the gap has now widened beyond repair.

But I’d argue that, given the solid numbers AMD is posting, it is not necessary nor justified to continue the comparisons between the two firms.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 24% YoY revenue growth to $7.7 billion, coming in at the high end of guidance of $7.5 billion (plus or minus $300 million). The company was profitable on a GAAP basis and saw non-GAAP EPS jump 42% YoY due to operating leverage.

The company drove growth primarily from the data center segment, which makes up more than half of overall revenues. Data center revenues grew 69% YoY and 9% sequentially. These are great numbers, but greatly lag the 93% YoY and 15% QoQ growth seen at NVDA. The company continued to see weakness in the gaming and embedded segments, but as management signaled in the third quarter, I expect these to see a pronounced cyclical recovery in addition to lapping easier comparables.

AMD ended the quarter with $5.1 billion of cash versus $1.7 billion of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for roughly $7.1 billion in revenue in the first quarter, representing 30% YoY growth and a 7% sequential decline. I suspect that some investors may have been disappointed by the latter point. While AMD has historically seen seasonal declines between these quarters, a true “Nvidia moment” would have presumably negated any seasonality.

On the conference call, management noted that:

IBM, Digital Ocean, Vultr and several other AI-focused CSPs have begun deploying AMD Instinct accelerators for new instances.

I think the fact that management is highlighting (in my opinion) tier 2 cloud operator customers sums up the competitive landscape quite well. Management appeared to give soft guidance of expanding their AI data center revenues from $5 billion in 2024 to “tens of billions of dollars of annual revenue over the coming years.” They repeated that target several times on the call, including as a response to an analyst question with regard to whether they can grow these revenues by 60% in 2025 as well. My guess is that management is (rightfully so) trying to manage analyst expectations for their AI business, which is unlikely to follow the same trajectory as that of NVDA.

Is AMD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, AMD traded hands at just over 20x this year’s earnings.

That looks like a cheap multiple given consensus estimates calling for solid double-digit growth over the coming years.

I'd note that estimates might prove optimistic in the near term due to blowback from tariffs, even if AMD might not be so directly affected given the exclusion of semiconductor chips from tariffs. I would not be surprised to see IT spending pullback in the near term due to heightened uncertainty.

On the other hand, I view the growth of AI, data, and all things data center to be unstoppable over the long term. Analyst expectations call for net margins to hit around 31% by 2029, which looks quite conservative in my view. With the stock trading at a price to earnings growth ratio ("PEG ratio") under 1x, it seems to me that Wall Street is pricing in the possibility that the growth story gets disrupted. I view this as unlikely as I can always see a market for lower-cost GPUs. The presence of such a formidable competitor might put a ceiling on valuation multiples, but I see a case for this stock to re-rate to around a 1.2x to 1.5x PEG ratio, equating to around 24x to 30x earnings or 7.2x to 9x sales (based on a 30% long-term net margin assumption).

AMD Stock Risks

If the AI story on the whole falls apart, then AMD can see material financial impact. While it is unclear if AMD will see more or less impact than NVDA in such a scenario, I think it will very likely see considerable impact regardless. It is possible that AMD really is being disrupted by NVDA and that growth will be far lower than analysts expect. In this case, I would expect the stock to re-rate downward due to it earning a more cyclical versus secular multiple. I expect AMD stock to face considerable volatility in a recession, but the solid GAAP profitability may give it the financial firepower to repurchase stock at lower levels. I am of the view that a lot of the pessimism has already been priced into the stock, as the recent volatility might not imply the same moving forward.

AMD Stock Conclusion

In comparison with NVDA, AMD is clearly struggling to benefit from anything close to the same growth seen in AI. Even so, the company is still posting respectable growth rates alongside GAAP profitability and a net cash balance sheet. I argue that the low valuation means that the company can underperform NVDA and still allow for strong upside ahead. AMD's strong top-line growth rates, net cash balance sheet, and undervaluation explain why it is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

data.title: Roche: Forget Tariffs And Focus On The Positives -- There Are Many
data.description:
Investment Overview

The Trump administration's tariffs came, created mayhem, and then took 90 days off — at least the more severe levies, and excepting China. It has been one of the more bizarre economic policies, implemented on "Liberation Day", and swiftly withdrew as stock markets globally began to crash.

The pharmaceuticals industry has been protected from tariffs for over 30 years thanks to an agreement orchestrated by the World Trade Organisation ("WTO") but the President insisted a couple of days ago their exemption was about to come to an end in the US.

President Trump has been quoted as saying they will arrive "very soon", and allegedly told reporters they would be "at a level that you haven't really seen before", and may have suggested they will be >25%. The rationale for tariffs on Pharma? According to President Trump:

We’re going to put tariffs on our pharmaceuticals and once we do that, they’re going to come rushing back into our country because we’re the big market.

The advantage we have over everybody is that we’re the big market. So we’re going to be announcing very shortly a major tariff on pharmaceuticals.

Unsurprisingly, Pharma stocks have been tanking as a result of the tariff threat - my research suggests -8% on a one-week basis, and -13% on a one-month basis across the fifteen or so largest.

Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY) shares are down nearly 20% on a one-month basis, and the company's market cap has dropped <$200bn as a result, which is somewhat low for a company that generated $68.7bn of revenues last year, operating income of $21.6bn, and earnings per share of $11.45 on a GAAP basis, and $20.3 on an adjusted basis, which translates to a price to earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~12x.

Given the ephemeral nature of the tariffs imposed on countries, however, should the Pharma industry be genuinely fearful of a >25% tariff on exports to the US, or should we instead think about how cheap European Pharma stocks are today and consider this a good time to buy? In this post, I'll tease out some ways to think about the opportunity — or lack of it.

Is A Tariff On Pharma Exports To The US Genuinely Realistic?

There's no question that European Pharmas are concerned about the imposition and impact of tariffs on exports to the US. Almost as soon as the news broke on Tuesday evening that Pharmas may be next in line for a levy, The European Federation of Pharmaceutical Industries and Associations (EFPIA), of which Roche is a member, alongside other Euro Pharma giants such as Novartis AG (NVS), Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), GSK plc (GSK), and US Pharmas including e.g., Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), Pfizer Inc. (PFE), and Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) met with the European Commission president, Ursula von der Leyen, and issued a press release stating that:

unless Europe delivers rapid, radical policy change then pharmaceutical research, development, and manufacturing are increasingly likely to be directed towards the US

The press release added:

The US now leads Europe on every investor metric from availability of capital, intellectual property, and speed of approval to rewards for innovation. In addition to the uncertainty created by the threat of tariffs, there is little incentive to invest in the EU and significant drivers to relocate to the US.

Clearly, the European Pharma industry is now concerned about companies opting to manufacture in the US as opposed to Europe - which is exactly what the Trump administration says it wants.

Logistically, however, imposing tariffs would be an administrative nightmare. Drugs are typically assembled in multiple countries, and apparently research suggests 90% of US biotechs rely on imported components. It won't be practically possible for drug manufacturers to switch all of their production to a single country for many years, and in the meantime, there is nothing stopping EU companies from simply increasing their prices to ensure they are not losing out.

Given how quickly tariffs on countries have been paused, we may speculate that the end-game, as far as the Trump administration is concerned, is focused on smaller goals — for example, encouraging drug manufacturers who are domiciled in Ireland for tax purposes and also manufacturing product there to return to the US, or potentially, an attempt to force EU countries to hike drug prices, which are often as much as 10x cheaper in the region than they are in the US.

Hence, the first thing to consider the mooted tariffs on Pharmas is that the threat will be withdrawn as soon as certain specific goals are achieved, i.e., commitments from certain countries to do more manufacturing overseas.

How Badly Would Roche Be Affected By Tariffs?

Back in February, Thomas Schinecker, the CEO of Roche told a television program "we are relatively relaxed because we have the entire value chain on site in the US", adding:

No, we already have a lot in the US. President Trump wants people or companies to invest in the US. And we are already well-invested.

Roche apparently makes its >$7bn per annum multiple sclerosis therapy Ocrevus entirely in the US and has ~25k staff in the US. The Pharma does the majority of its business in the US, also, earnings of CHF 24.774m in the region in 2024 - ~$30.5bn, versus ~$11bn in the EU, and ~$11.9bn international ex-Japan.

It seems to me, therefore, not out of the question that Roche could even benefit from Pharma tariffs, as it has a lot of flexibility, although it is highly doubtful that Pharma would advocate for tariffs. Even Eli Lilly's CEO, despite recently committing to spending ~$27bn on four new manufacturing sites in the US, has declared himself against tariffs.

Nevertheless, my suspicion is that Roche and its business are not the reason the Trump administration is threatening tariffs or the ultimate target of tariffs - it is not even headquartered in the EU, but in Switzerland, although the business may now be regretting the $1.2bn sale of its Genentech manufacturing facility in Vacaville, California, to Lonza, last year.

Furthermore, ~25% of Roche's business is diagnostics, and it's unclear if this would be as badly affected by tariffs as pharmaceutical drug products - my suspicion would be diagnostics products could be more readily assembled in a single country. Apparently, the company has said in a statement that it has a full diagnostics value chain in the US, but that it has been "considering additional U.S. investments to continue to meet patients’ needs".

Roche Is Not In Generics, It Is In High-Margin Pharmaceuticals With An Exciting Pipeline

This is an important point as generic drugmakers are typically lower margin and manufactured in regions like India or China, which may be a more specific target for President Trump's tariffs.

In 2024, Roche marketed and sold ten "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) oncology drugs within a division that earned >$25.8bn, 2 blockbusters including Ocrevus in its >$10bn neuroscience division, two within its ~$7bn immunology division, one in its >$4.3bn ophthalmology division, and earned ~$16bn from diagnostics products.

Meddling with the manufacturing of these products could create global drug shortages, which is a scenario the Trump administration would wish to avoid, I'd speculate, although it's possible that Roche may have to sacrifice some margin if tariffs were introduced.

Shareholders may not be happy about that, but Roche's net profit margin in 2024 of ~14% is fairly healthy, as is its dividend payout ratio of >4%, which would only increase if the share price declines. Management has said it is committed to growing the dividend in 2025 (source: earnings presentation).

As we can see above, Roche expects to roll several more products with "blockbuster" potential off of its production line in 2025, mostly targeted at the US market, therefore, tariffs or no tariffs, Roche remains a company with exciting growth potential, which is good news for its bottom line as well as its top line.

Perhaps the biggest potential upside catalyst for Roche's valuation longer-term is the investments the company has been making into developing weight loss/obesity drugs. Roche's $2.7bn acquisition of Carmot Therapeutics in 2023 gave it access to three GLP-1 agonist drug candidates - which work similarly to tirzepatide, developed by Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) and marketed and sold as Mounjaro to treat Type 2 diabetes and Zepbound to treat obesity, and semaglutide, developed by Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) and marketed and sold in the same indications as Ozempic / Wegovy.

These are Phase 2 stage CT-388, a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist injected subcutaneously, CT-996, a Phase 1 stage oral, small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist, and CT-868, a Phase-2, once-daily subcutaneous injectable, dual GLP-1/GIP receptor focused on the treatment of Type 1 diabetes (“T1D”).

Furthermore, Roche has entered into a collaboration and licensing agreement with Zealand Pharma, in order to:

Co-develop and co-commercialise petrelintide, Zealand Pharma’s amylin analog as a standalone therapy as well as a fixed-dose combination with Roche’s lead incretin asset CT-388.

Petrelintide, currently in phase 2 clinical development, is a long-acting amylin analog suitable for once-weekly subcutaneous administration. The available clinical data suggests its potential to become a best-in-class amylin monotherapy, with improved tolerability compared to current weight management treatments and to expand into adjacent indications.

The combination of petrelintide with Roche’s dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist CT-388 will further strengthen and expand Roche’s pipeline in the field of cardiovascular, renal, and metabolic (CVRM) diseases. This combination offers the opportunity for best-in-disease efficacy while potentially offering enhanced tolerability.

With an impressive armoury of GLP-1 agonist drugs, it's possible that Roche could launch the next major obesity drug, which is an exciting prospect, as tirzepatide/semaglutide generated >$40bn revenues between them in 2024, and the industry is anticipated to be worth >$200bn by 2030, with $70bn coming from new challenger drugs.

Concluding Thoughts: Near-Term Volatility Is Guaranteed, But The Long-Term Outlook Is Still Positive, In My View

As discussed in this post, there are several reasons why I suspect the bear run on Roche stock in recent weeks and months may well be reversed in time:

  1. Pharma tariffs may not be introduced at all;
  2. Even if they are, Roche's exposure would not be excessive and the company has the infrastructure and flexibility to make the best of it;
  3. The Pharma has a strong, profitable business, buoyed by near-term drug launches;
  4. The company could be the next major player in the commercial GLP-1 / obesity space.

Yes, there are some "ifs, buts and maybes" to consider with this thesis - if the US heads into a recession, there will likely be no Pharma winners, negative market sentiment may swallow up the entire Pharma segment, and pipeline drugs must be converted into commercial success stories, which is a tricky, unpredictable business that can ultimately waste billions of R&D dollars.

Roche's stock price performance has not been great in recent years either, down ~22% on a five-year basis at the time of writing, but the dividend compensates slightly for this, and there is positive momentum - stock is up 10% on a 1-year basis despite the heavy one-month loss.

Personally, I am long Roche stock, and I am not considering selling, and contemplating whether now is the right time to accumulate more. Readers will doubtless have their own opinions, and as mentioned, volatility is king at present, but I suspect Roche is resilient enough to come through this bear market and create value in the longer term.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta: Market Turmoil To Create Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Author’s Preamble

I have an actively managed investment portfolio, and I regularly trade stocks within my investing universe (or watch list) depending upon the stock’s price relative to my estimate of its intrinsic value and its market trading patterns (technical indicators).

I share my valuations with readers to get feedback and to gain new insights from other knowledgeable investors.

I last wrote about Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) in a July 2023 article with the title Meta Platforms: Fairly Priced But Upcoming Earnings Could Move the Dial.

A lot has changed since then, so let’s dive into an update.

Company Description

Most of the company’s revenues come from selling advertising placements. META has two reportable operating segments – Facebook Family of Apps (which comprises the advertising business and the other revenues collected from the social network business) and the virtual reality business (Reality Labs).

At the end of FY 2024, the reported revenues and operating margins by division were:

META reports that 38% of revenues come from North America, 23% from Europe, 27% from Asia-Pacific and 12% from the rest of the world.

Investors should note that the Reality Labs division is yet to report an operating profit, and operating losses have been increasing year-on-year with negligible revenue growth.

Business Overview

Digital Advertising Market

There is some uncertainty about the exact size of the global digital advertising market. It is relatively trivial to sum up the reported advertising revenues of the listed companies in the sector (Google, Meta, Amazon, etc.) but other major players (Alibaba and TikTok) choose not to report their advertising revenues.

My estimate of the sector’s historical revenues is shown on the following chart:

Statista estimates that at the end of FY 2024 the global market had revenues of $791 billion and was expected to grow at 6% to 7% per year for the next five years.

There is little doubt that the digital advertising market is starting to mature and, based on the law of large numbers, the sector’s growth rate should begin to taper, but I think that Statista’s near-term estimate of the sector’s growth is potentially too pessimistic.

The sector is dominated by a small number of companies, but particularly by Google and Meta, who I estimate have a combined market share of 54%.

In previous reports I have provided a snapshot of the digital advertising revenues for the major players in the sector, and this is my latest update:

In aggregate, changes to the macroeconomic environment caused by government responses to the COVID pandemic were very positive for the sector, but the data indicates that there were both winners and losers. It has taken a year or two for the impact to be “washed out” and a new normal growth trajectory established.

The situation for META was made more complex as they were also dealing with the impact of the changes Apple made to the privacy settings on their iPhones during FY 2021. This threatened the quality of information that META provided to its advertisers and may have been a significant contributor to the decline in its advertising revenues at the time.

The fall in advertising revenue growth during the second half of FY 2021 triggered a collapse in the META share price (it declined by 76% from August 2021 to October 2022) and forced META to rethink its business strategy.

META executed a restructuring during Q3 FY 2022 which resulted in the consolidation of facilities, changes in its data center strategy and the termination of 11,000 employees. Over FY 2022 and FY 2023, META incurred a total restructuring charge of $6,900 M.

Since its restructuring, META’s advertising financial performance has dramatically improved.

The following chart shows the quarterly changes in reported daily active people using the Facebook Family of Apps since Q1 FY 2020:

The following chart shows Meta’s trailing 12-month Family of Apps revenue and annual growth rate, updated quarterly since Q1 FY 2020:

At the end of Q4 FY 2024, daily active user numbers were growing at 5% per year, whilst revenue per user grew at 16% per year.

META’s user growth is reasonably close to the projected growth for the sector, but the monetization growth is spectacularly higher. I think that this demonstrates the value that Meta is continuing to provide for its advertisers, which is enabling it to raise prices.

A key outcome of the restructuring was a more efficient organization, with a greater commitment to the advertising business and less focus on the virtual reality division.

The recovery in advertising growth has been spectacular.

Virtual & Augmented Reality Market

The virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) market comprises hardware, device controlling software and content. For some time, there have been wide discrepancies amongst analysts about the size of the VR/AR market and its long-term market growth potential.

I am not confident in the estimates about this market from the various market consultants.

META acquired its core VR investment in FY 2014 when it purchased Oculus for $2 Billion. This investment has been supported by additional relatively small acquisitions of VR software companies over the last few years.

The following chart shows the trailing 12-month revenues for Reality Labs:

The concerning issue is that revenues appear to have peaked during Q2 of 2022 and have not recovered. The division is yet to report an operating profit and the year-on-year losses are increasing.

An article in the Financial Times suggests that META has invested almost $100 Billion in Reality Labs. Although META reports the division’s revenues and operating losses, it doesn’t identify the amount of capital invested in the division. By simply summing the reported operating losses from FY 2019 to FY 2024 generates a total of $69 Billion (admittedly there may be non-cash items included in this figure) but it indicates that the Financial Times estimate is potentially dimensionally correct.

I have grave concerns that META is over-investing in this market and unless the long-term market potential increases substantially I think that it may be difficult for META to generate an acceptable long-term return on investment.

Company Life Cycle

META has been a public company for almost 13 years. Technology companies reputedly age in “dog years”. At the end of Q1 FY 2024, META, for the first time, paid a dividend to its shareholders.

According to Professor Damodaran’s Corporate Life Cycle model, this is a sign that META has entered the mature growth phase. The payment of a dividend reflects that the company no longer needs all the cash that it is generating to support the future growth of its existing businesses.

The global regulatory environment is becoming more complex for companies like META. I suspect that this means any materially significant future acquisitions will not gain regulatory approval. This means that all future revenue growth must be organic.

The key question is: Can the Reality Labs division become the next growth platform for META, or will Mark Zuckerberg be forced to admit that he was wrong, which may cause the company to have a midlife crisis of confidence?

Meta’s Historical Financial Performance

Revenues and Operating Margins

Meta’s historical revenues and adjusted operating margins are shown in the chart below:

The reported operating margins have been adjusted for the impact of:

  • Operating leases (converting the lease payments to debt and depreciation).
  • Research & Development expenses by eliminating this as an operating expense (this should really be treated as a capital investment) and I have replaced it with an R&D amortization charge (I have assumed that the R&D investments have a 3-year life).
  • Capitalizing 20% of the expensed Sales & Marketing charge and turning it into an investment with a 3-year life (I assume that this portion of the expense is about promoting the brand for the long term).
  • Identifiable extraordinary expenses.

The chart clearly shows the additional margin that has been generated since the internal restructuring which took place in FY 2022.

Readers should be aware that the reported margin is being dragged down by the performance of the Reality Labs division. The underlying adjusted margin of the Family of Apps division is currently at a 15-year high.

I estimate that if META eliminated the losses from Reality Labs, the FY 2024 company’s adjusted operating margin would be 60%.

Capital Structure

I have previously commented on META’s evolution to becoming a mature company. This is reflected in the changes to the capital structure. More debt is being taken on and is replacing higher cost equity in the capital stack. These are normal changes expected from a maturing company and are not a cause for concern.

The following chart shows the historical changes in META’s capital structure:

As META continues to mature, I would expect that the levels of debt and the debt ratio will continue to rise.

Cash Flows

The following table summarizes META’s cash flow for the last 10 years:

Readers will note that I have made some adjustments to Meta’s published financial statements to make clear what the net operating cash flows from existing assets are and to clarify what reinvestment is being made to maintain and grow the business.

Of all the companies that I analyze, Meta has the highest restated operating cash flow to sales ratio by a wide margin. This is testament to the tremendous profitability of the company’s operations.

Over the last 10 years, META has generated $645.7 billion in adjusted operating cash flow. It has reinvested $398.4 billion back into the business (with very little in the way of acquisitions). Meta has a very high level of reinvestment to sales compared to many of its peers. The level of reinvestment has significantly stepped higher since the pandemic.

R&D and capital spending consumes 94% of the total reinvestment (only 1% is spent on acquisitions, and the remainder is spent on brand building).

The following chart shows the reinvestment trend:

The reinvestment increase can be attributed to Meta’s development of AI applications. Mark Zuckerberg has declared that Meta is positioning itself to be an AI market leader.

META has indicated that in FY 2024 it spent around $39 Billion on AI, and this is expected to increase in FY 2025 to $62 Billion. It is investing in data centers, GPU chip sets and the development of multimodal large language models.

Meta is developing several AI models, with Llama AI being the foundational tool for both consumer-facing applications and research (the current version is Llama 4, which is an open-source application which can be used by third-party developers).

Meta has had some early wins with its AI investment as it is believed that AI has been successfully used to respond to the Apple iOS privacy changes which threatened the profitability of the advertising business.

Over time, the level of reinvestment should decline (particularly as the company continues to maturity), thus providing another source of additional cash for distribution to shareholders.

Over the last 10 years Meta has taken on a small amount of new debt ($29.1 billion) which meant that there was $195.4 billion of free cash generated. A total of $152.8 billion was distributed back to shareholders, predominantly in the form of share buybacks, and the remaining cash was banked.

I expect that dividend payments will increase year-on-year and there should continue to be ample excess cash available for share buybacks.

Return on Invested Capital

The following chart shows the history of Meta’s return on invested capital [ROIC] over the last 10 years:

Note that I have adjusted the published financial data for:

  • One-off extraordinary expenses
  • Operating leases
  • Capitalization of research & development expenses
  • Partial capitalization of branding expenses
  • Adding back to the capital base all asset write-offs

Meta’s ROIC is both high and stable, which leads me to conclude that its relative competitive position to date has been very strong.

As I noted previously, there are estimates by some media commentators that Meta has invested in the order of $100 billion into its Reality Labs division. This is having a significant drag on the ROIC, which makes the returns from the advertising business even more impressive.

My Investment Thesis for Meta

Regulatory story

I believe that the current political/regulatory environment is the source of Meta’s most significant risks. Meta is forced to keep “onside” with the major country governments throughout the world to maintain its local license to operate.

It has been reported that during the COVID pandemic, many governments pressured META to moderate some content on its apps. Evidently, this pressure continues to happen today. Clearly, this is contentious for several reasons – governments change over time (opposition parties form government) and app users become resentful that their free speech is being censored.

Meta’s management is mindful that governments can issue meaningful penalties (including fines, the threat of a company break-up and the ability to prevent acquisitions) for regulatory non-compliance. This threat has severe potential implications for Meta’s future growth prospects.

Meta has skillfully avoided seriously upsetting its regulators and users, but I suspect that this will become more difficult over time. European regulators regularly pursue technology companies (particularly U.S.-based) for breaches, which generally lead to significant financial penalties. I expect that this will continue.

My scenario consequently restricts Meta’s ability to grow through acquisitions. I have also discounted the impact of a forced breakup / demerger of META because I don’t believe that it would impact the valuation on a sum of the parts basis (although it may impact the future growth and profitability potential of the individual apps).

Reality Labs – Important strategic issue

At some point, Meta must face up to the performance of the Reality Labs division. Does it continue to invest in it and hope that the division will grow revenues and earnings sufficiently to become a meaningful contributor to the company’s future, or should the division be sold/spun off?

If I were the decision-maker, I would sell the business and take the financial write-downs. This would allow the company to focus solely on the digital advertising market. The company could then focus on the optimization of its internal processes as it prepared for a lower growth/maturing market.

I suspect that it would be too embarrassing for Mark Zuckerberg to admit that Reality Labs was a strategic error. For this reason, my scenario includes a revenue and margin projection for the division as a going concern.

Growth Story

The digital advertising market is maturing and is only expected to grow at around 6% per year for the next five years. META’s daily active subscriber growth is currently 5% per year, but its subscriber monetization growth rate is much higher (currently 16%).

I expect that Meta's AI investments will enable it to continue to grow revenues faster than the sector average, but the growth rate will moderate. As a result, FOA's revenues can grow at 10% to 15% per year for the next five years before slowly declining to the terminal rate after 10 years.

It is more difficult to project the revenue growth for the Reality Labs division because of the link between revenues and new product releases. For simplicity, I am forecasting that the division’s revenues can grow at 15% per year for the next five years before declining to the terminal rate after 10 years.

Margin Story

The reported FOA's margin is currently around 54% - even allowing for the impact of expensing of R&D this is very high compared to the rest of the sector. I expect that margins are close to peaking and that over time there will be a decline due to competitive forces and the general maturity of the sector. I am projecting that FOA’s long-term margins will be about 41%.

The more contentious issue is, where will the Reality Labs margins settle? I am assuming that margins can gradually improve over time. I am projecting that the division will break even by FY 2027 and that long-term margins will be around 15% (about mid-way between hardware and software sector margins).

Due to the expected long-term disparity between the size of the two divisions, Meta’s overall long-term margins will continue to be closer to the FOA margin.

Growth Efficiency

I have previously discussed that Meta reinvests heavily back into its business to sustain its revenue growth. After adjusting for the expensing of R&D, I estimate that it currently generates 85 cents in revenue for every one dollar reinvested. The median company in this sector has a higher ratio of 1.2.

Over time, the rate of reinvestment will decline, and I have estimated that the long-term ratio will be 1.5 (which is more reflective of a mature company in the sector).

Risk Story

I use the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) as a proxy for reflecting the level of risk associated with a company’s future cash flows (the higher the risk, the higher the cost of capital).

Equity markets over the last two weeks have become very volatile. The rising volatility combined with concerns about the strength of the global economy has led to a sharp increase in the equity risk premium (a key input into a company’s cost of capital).

Using Professor Damodaran’s process, I estimate that today’s equity risk premium is 5.0% (his estimate at the start of April was 4.6%). An increase in the equity risk premium causes a company’s intrinsic value to fall (provided that all other inputs stay constant).

I estimate that Meta’s current cost of capital is 9.7% (this reflects Meta’s current capital structure and the sector’s volatility). My current estimate of the median U.S. listed non-financial company’s cost of capital is 8.5%. Over time, I would expect Meta’s terminal cost of capital to move to the market median.

Competitive Advantages

Meta’s technology investment has generated a very strong competitive position. I expect that Meta’s ongoing reinvestment in its business will ensure that this position is maintained in the future. It is inevitable that the company’s ROIC will decline over time, but I expect that it will always be significantly higher than its cost of capital.

Key Assumptions in Meta’s Valuation

The following table summarizes the key inputs into the valuation:

Discounted Cash Flow Valuation

The output from my DCF model is:

I also developed a Monte Carlo simulation for the valuation based on the range of inputs for the valuation. The output of the simulation is developed after 100,000 iterations.

The Monte Carlo simulation is very useful in helping to understand the major sources of sensitivity in the valuation.

In this valuation, the major influence on the output is the estimate for revenue growth over the next five years.

The simulation indicates that, based on my future scenario, the valuation for META is between $484 and $704 per share with an expected value around $582.

I think that based on my scenario, META is currently priced in the range of fair value.

Final Summary

Analysts are projecting that the digital advertising sector’s revenues will grow at around 6% to 7% for the next five years. This is the trajectory of a maturing sector. Meta continues to grow revenues faster than most of their peers, but interestingly, their subscriber numbers are growing at a similar rate to the sector's forecast. This suggests that a large portion of META’s growth is coming from raising prices.

Prices are rising because the value being provided is also rising. META is investing significant amounts in AI to maintain its market position and has signaled that this will continue in the near term. As a result, I expect that META will be able to sustain a growth rate above most of its competitors.

What have been the returns to long-term shareholders?

META has been an excellent long-term investment, but there have been short periods when it has underperformed relative to its peers, as shown in the following table:

Meta and its peers have significantly and consistently outperformed relative to the broader market.

A contrarian may believe at some point the sector will underperform, but I don’t think that we are there yet, and I am confident that in the near term the sector (and META) will continue to do well.

The current market volatility may present an opportunity for investors to buy META and its peers at a 12-month low.

What could possibly go wrong?

The key to my estimate of Meta’s intrinsic value is the expected revenue growth of 12.5% per year over the next five years. If revenue growth were to fall to 10% per year, for instance, then the valuation falls from $582 to $516 (all other things being equal).

This is the metric to watch.

What do Seeking Alpha’s Quant factor grades suggest?

The most recent data from Seeking Alpha:

The SA summary suggests that although extremely profitable, Meta is currently overpriced relative to its peers. I note that the SA analysis is based on relative valuation metrics and is a comparison between META and the sector average.

My analysis is that META is not expensive.

What trading are insiders doing in META stock?

The data indicates that insiders NEVER buy META shares anymore. They are consistent stock sellers as shown by the following chart:

Even Mark Zuckerberg has been a constant seller of stock. According to GuruFocus, Zuckerberg so far during FY 2025 has sold 1.1 million shares, with net proceeds of $733 million from the trades.

Is Meta a Buy, Hold or Sell Today?

I am a believer in using fundamental analysis in determining the long-term direction of a company’s stock price. I expect that over the long term, the price will move to the company’s intrinsic value.

However, I also believe that in the near term, price movements are best predicted using technical analysis tools. Although I am not proficient in the use of technical tools, I can reasonably determine when a stock is oversold and over bought.

META’s most recent highest closing price was on February 14, 2025. At the time, the price chart appeared to indicate that the stock was significantly over-bought (high RSI and falling MACD indicators). This was the time to take some profits, notwithstanding that hindsight is never wrong.

Since that time, the stock price has fallen by 26%. Currently, META’s price action is being driven by market sentiment. The price may go lower, or it may consolidate before trading higher – the direction will be controlled by factors external to the company.

I am a frequent trader of META stock, but I currently do not hold any stock. My advice to existing stockholders is the time has passed to sell, and I would continue to HOLD my stock.

I am hoping that the current market turmoil pushes the price lower so that I can buy back at a price below my estimate of intrinsic value.

data.title: Y-mAbs Therapeutics: A Yawn From The Market Means Opportunity
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

At the beginning of 2025, I wrote an article highlighting what, I felt, were some key strengths for the investment thesis of Y-mAbs Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:YMAB), a biotech focused on developing novel antibody-based treatments for cancer. The stock has since continued a steady decline amid political and economic uncertainty, but does that explain everything about why YMAB is underappreciated, or is there something going wrong with the hypothesis? Let's have another look to see how the story is evolving for this company.

Pipeline Updates

Naxitamab

YMAB continues to commercialize and develop a market for their GD2 antibody naxitamab as part of treatment for children with high-risk neuroblastoma. While in my last article, there was not much to highlight on the scientific front, back in March YMAB announced the publication of an interim data readout of their phase 2 study in relapsed/refractory neuroblastoma with residual bone disease.

This was Trial 201, the confirmatory study for the approved indication. These findings showed a 50% overall response rate, most of the responses being complete (no evidence of disease present). These findings continue to confirm the evidence of the activity of naxitamab in this population.

GD2-SADA and CD38-SADA

As part of their strategic realignment, YMAB is focusing most of its remaining R&D efforts on the development of a radiopharmaceutical platform called SADA. Here, the company takes an antibody directed against a target of interest (in these cases, either GD2 or CD38, the latter being an important target in multiple myeloma), and affixes a radioisotope to the molecule with the hope of delivering highly targeted radiation therapy specifically to tumor expressing the target of interest.

It's a technique that's been put to famous use in GI neuroendocrine tumors and prostate cancer, and while the fever pitch for conjugated antibodies has settled somewhat, there remains substantial interest in exploring novel targeted cytotoxic approaches like these.

We're still awaiting a formal clinical readout of the first-in-human study for GD2-SADA, which is anticipated in the coming weeks (management has previously guided a Q2 2025 top-line cut), but the company has presented preclinical findings recently. At the SNMMI mid-winter meeting, YMAB showed off translational pharmacokinetics that illustrate the potential for the self-assembling and disassembling molecules to improve tumor exposure while ensuring timely plasma elimination of the agent.

These findings informed the design of the phase 1 ongoing study, so I am looking forward to seeing a formal readout in the near future. A phase 1 study for CD38-SADA is ongoing, as well, with an update from management on the 2024 annual earnings call showing that 5 sites are activated, and the company is working to dose the first patient.

Financial Overview

As of their 2024 annual filing, YMAB held $67.2 million in cash and equivalents. They had $85.2 million in product revenue, similar to the $84.3 million in FY2023. Gross profit was $72.7 million, and operating expenses reached $103.9 million for the year. The net loss was $29.7 million, up from $21.4 million the year prior.

Actual cash used for the year was $15.7 million, and at this cash burn rate, YMAB has an estimated cash runway of around 4 years. Of course, this does not account for the continued decline in sales or increase in costs, which management has projected will increase to between $116 million and $125 million in 2025 as they ramp up the SADA research.

Management have also guided that their cash position is anticipated to be sufficient to fund operations "into 2027."

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Steady sales of an approved product supporting aggressive expansion

Although sales of naxitamab have more or less flattened in 2024, with no clear signs that the company will be able to achieve further growth (and their own guidance on revenue is pretty far-ranging and conservative), it is important to note that this is the platform they're building out a larger story on, and these revenues are highly supportive of the rather large cost of doing research.

Normally, a company with only around $60 million in liquidity would give you serious pause, as it is indicative of an upcoming serious cash concern. But with the offset, YMAB can comfortably continue their projects through 2025 and most of 2026 without needing to worry about financing.

This offers them the opportunity to conduct any cash raises from a position of strength, and important updates to the SADA studies in 2025 will give us some clarity on the feasibility of this project.

Risk - SADA's success is far from guaranteed

I've highlighted this risk several times, including in my most recent article. We have seen very, very little from the SADA story, and it is pretty much the entire foundation for an investment thesis in YMAB right now, barring any sort of unexpected expansion of naxitamab sales.

Practically, nothing about SADA is guaranteed at this time, other than the fact their first target, GD2, is firmly established as a viable molecule to exploit for the treatment of neuroblastoma. Validation of the platform is going to require navigating toxicity concerns in addition to demonstrating that the efficacy is worth the trouble of developing this complex new approach. This puts a lot of pressure on a phase 1 readout, which is not something I'm normally very comfortable with.

Bottom Line Summary

Heading into Q2 2025, YMAB has continued a valuation decline, and at this time, there are no clear signals that this is due to poor confidence in YMAB specifically. Rather, their decline is consistent with broader market moves that have seen some unusually poor confidence from investors across all of biotech.

This is a signal that there are deep bargains being developed by the market as we speak. Obviously, no one knows exactly what those are, or when the pendulum might swing back, but companies with a relatively solid early hypothesis would seem to be prime candidates for appreciation at some point.

YMAB is one of these, in my view. If they can show validation for the SADA platform with any evidence of activity, they may gain substantially in the short term. However, this upcoming update is key for the longer-term viability of SADA and the future of the investment thesis. YMAB continues to be one to keep on your watchlist, if not being worthy of a small position in a balanced portfolio.

I maintain a "Buy" sentiment here, recognizing the risks involved. But their long cash runway and multiple opportunities for growth make them a company I'm not comfortable ignoring.
